title: Negative equity looms for recent homebuyers
full_text:
The housing market is under fire from several directions at once, and the country’s biggest lender thinks it will only get worse before it gets better.
In its latest forecasts, Commonwealth Bank predicts national dwelling prices will be flat this year – down from a forecast of 3 per cent at budget time and 5 per cent in March.
More concerning for the Sydney and Melbourne markets, which account for about 40 per cent of the nation’s housing stock, CBA is predicting falls of 6 per cent and 7 per cent respectively this year, potentially enough to tip some recent first-home buyers into negative equity.
Perth and Brisbane are a different story. They are forecast to rise 12 per cent and 8 per cent respectively, underpinned by years of population growth outpacing new supply.
It comes as economists continue to slash their house price forecasts amid rapid-fire interest rate rises from the Reserve Bank, tighter lending conditions, stretched affordability and the shock to sentiment from the federal government’s negative gearing and capital gains tax changes.
Auction clearance rates have been falling since the RBA began its recent rising cycle, and the median time for properties on the market has risen.
The budget reforms limit negative gearing to new builds only, and replace the 50 per cent CGT discount with cost-base indexation and a 30 per cent minimum tax rate.
CBA now expects house prices to eventually settle just under 5 per cent below where they otherwise would have been – up from a prior estimate of 3 per cent – because most investors relied on negative gearing to fund holding costs and are unlikely to value the ability to quarantine losses.
That echoes Morgan Stanley’s view this week that established property prices may need to fall 15-20 per cent to restore the after-tax economics investors previously enjoyed.
CBA does predict a rebound in house prices in 2027 and 2028, contingent on its forecast that interest rates won’t go any higher and the RBA will deliver rate cuts in May and August 2027.
However, the bank warns that the “risks to this outlook are tilted towards the cash rate remaining higher for longer, as reflected in current market pricing.” If the cash rate were instead to remain at its current level through 2027, the recovery in dwelling prices next year would be “more subdued”.
Indeed, the concern is that rates could go even higher. Westpac tips two more rate increases this year, NAB one more, while ANZ sees no change.
Of course, house price falls carry real consequences for recent buyers. Based on CBA’s forecasts, a first homebuyer who purchased a $1.5m Sydney property – the maximum under the federal Home Guarantee Scheme – with a 5 per cent deposit at the start of the year could be left with just $2377 in equity by December, according to Canstar data insights director Sally Tindall.
In Melbourne, a 5 per cent deposit buyer of a $950,000 home could end up $7995 in the red.
“A buyer who purchased with a 5 per cent deposit at the start of this year has very little buffer against falling property prices,” Tindall says.
“Negative equity isn’t necessarily a crisis if you plan to stay put and keep making repayments, but it can become a major problem if you’re forced to sell or want to refinance. If you do find yourself in this position, don’t panic – put your head down and keep your mortgage repayments up.” Tindall is also quick to temper any optimism about affordability. “A 6 per cent or 7 per cent fall in house prices might sound significant, but when you’re talking about markets that have seen years of strong growth, combined with three cash rate hikes eroding borrowing capacity, it’s unlikely to suddenly make home ownership easy,” she says.
On the lending side, CBA expects investor loan numbers to fall to about half their 2025 fourth quarter levels.
Some major banks have already moved to exclude the current-year tax benefit from negative gearing when assessing borrowing capacity. Investor credit growth is expected to trough at 3.5 per cent in mid-2027, and owner-occupier credit growth at 5.5 per cent.
“What’s particularly striking is how divided the banks have become on the outlook for interest rates,” Tindall says.
“Borrowers should prepare for a range of scenarios rather than assuming rate relief is locked in.”
ANNUAL DWELLING PRICE GROWTH % Dec 2025 Dec 2026* Dec 2027* Sydney 6.4 -6 3 Melbourne 5.3 -7 3 Brisbane 14.7 8 4 Perth 17.2 12 4 Adelaide 8.5 6 3 Hobart 7 4 2 ACT 4.8 -2 3 Darwin 18.6 8 3 National 9 0 3 *Forecast Source: CBA, Cotality
